ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a daikon/white radish food entity but no specific entry for braised daikon with soy sauce. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for braised white radish with soy sauce. A00LC (radishes and similar root vegetables) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for braised white radish with soy sauce as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of braised white radish with soy sauce is approximately 200 g, consisting of several slices of tender braised daikon in a savoury soy-based broth, served as a side dish or accompaniment.

nutrient_source.title: Braised white radish with soy sauce — estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Daikon radish, raw (FDC ID 169276) and soy sauce (FDC ID 172238), adjusted for braising water absorption and soy sauce contribution
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for braised white radish with soy sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for raw daikon radish (FDC ID 169276) as the primary ingredient (~85% by weight) with soy sauce (FDC ID 172238) contribution (~15% by weight), adjusted for typical braising losses and water uptake. Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce. Energy and macros reflect the low-calorie, high-water nature of daikon. Values represent a reasonable estimate for a typical restaurant-style preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of vegetables — analogous estimate based on white radish / daikon; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised white radish with soy sauce. White radish (daikon) is a non-starchy vegetable with very low digestible carbohydrate content. Based on the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008), non-starchy root vegetables such as radish have a GI estimated at approximately 15 (low). The soy sauce broth contributes negligible additional glycemic load. GL per 200 g serving = (15 × 5.2 × 200/100) / 100 = 1.56, rounded to 1.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request a reduced-soy-sauce preparation, as a 200 g serving provides approximately 960 mg sodium, which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: White radish braised with soy sauce has a low GI of approximately 15 and provides only 5.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it a suitable vegetable side dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though sodium intake from the soy sauce broth should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium from soy sauce despite low fat and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While this dish is very low in fat, saturated fat, and cholesterol, the high sodium content from soy sauce (approximately 480 mg per 100 g) poses a concern for individuals with cardiovascular disease who require strict sodium restriction; a low-sodium soy sauce variant is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White Radish Braised with Soy Sauce (酱烧白萝卜) is a classic Chinese vegetable side dish in which thick slices of white radish (daikon, Raphanus sativus var. longipinnatus) are slow-simmered in a savoury broth made from soy sauce, water, sugar, garlic, and ginger until the radish becomes completely tender and absorbs the umami-rich flavour of the braising liquid. The dish is characterised by its melt-in-the-mouth texture, deep amber colour from the soy sauce, and a balance of savoury, slightly sweet, and subtly pungent flavours. It is commonly served as a side dish or accompaniment in Chinese home cooking and restaurant settings across Singapore and China. Nutritionally, braised white radish is very low in energy (approximately 28 kcal per 100 g), low in fat (0.2 g per 100 g), and provides modest dietary fibre (1.2 g per 100 g) and vitamin C (12 mg per 100 g). The primary nutritional concern is the elevated sodium content (approximately 480 mg per 100 g) contributed by the soy sauce, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is very low (approximately 15), making this dish suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free, but contains soy and gluten allergens from the soy sauce.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for braised white radish with soy sauce was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for raw daikon radish and soy sauce, adjusted for typical braising preparation. GI is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy vegetable GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary ingredient (daikon) is well-characterised in approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central (daikon radish FDC ID 169276 and soy sauce FDC ID 172238) as no exact entry for this prepared dish exists in any approved primary source. Values should be updated if an exact HPB Singapore Foodo or equivalent entry becomes available. 2. Sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce and is the primary nutritional concern for this dish. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin_b12 is naturally zero in plant foods. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as daikon and soy sauce contain no meaningful vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as no hydrogenated fats are used in this preparation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for the component ingredients. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as this is a fully plant-based dish; naturally zero. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimated from USDA daikon data. 8. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Vegetables' as white radish is the primary ingredient and the dish is a braised vegetable preparation. 9. GI set to 15 based on analogous estimate for non-starchy root vegetables per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). 10. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g fields are not present in this record; all micronutrient fields have been populated with estimated or confirmed values from USDA FoodData Central.
